claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit); Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! I'm here to help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit. Let me ask you some questions to assess your situation.

**First, let's start with information about your child:**

How old is the child you're claiming for? This will help me determine the right path for your assessment:

- **Under 16 years old**
- **Between 16 and 19 years old** 
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know your child's age (or which age category they fall into).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes I'm wondering if I can claim Child Benefit. I have one child - Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information! Let me help you with your Child Benefit claim for Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Since Alex is 8 years old (under 16), that's a good start. Now I need to check a few more things:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

For an 8-year-old, this is typically straightforward - most children this age are not in paid work. Can you confirm that Alex is not working 24 or more hours per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - he's only 8! He's in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That makes perfect sense! Now, the next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

This means benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that would be claimed in Alex's own name (not yours). For most 8-year-olds in primary school, this wouldn't apply.

Can you confirm that Alex is not receiving any benefits like ESA or Universal Credit in his own right?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name. He's just a regular 8-year-old at school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Next question:

**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

Since Alex is only 8 years old and in primary school, I expect the answer is no, but I need to confirm this to continue the assessment. Apprenticeships typically apply to older teenagers and young adults.

Can you confirm that Alex is not in an apprenticeship?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - he's only 8! He's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now let's move on to questions about you as the claimant. 

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if ANY of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support your family
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do you meet any of these residency requirements?** The most common is simply that you normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm here in England with Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to confirm:

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You meet this requirement if ANY of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex** 
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the amount of Child Benefit or equivalent in kind for food, clothes, housing, etc.)
3. You pay maintenance for Alex under a court order or agreement (at least equal to Child Benefit amount)

Based on what you've told me, it sounds like you live with Alex. Can you confirm that you live with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to check:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

This is important because only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. 

For example, this might happen if:
- Alex's other parent is claiming
- A grandparent or other family member is claiming
- There's a shared custody arrangement where someone else has already made a claim

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or is there no other claimant?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to understand Alex's living situation:

**Which of the following best describes Alex's current situation?**

- Alex is **in hospital**
- Alex is **in care** (looked after by the local authority)
- Alex is **fostered**
- Alex is **living normally** at home with you

Based on what you've told me so far (that Alex lives at home with you and goes to primary school), it sounds like Alex is living normally at home. Can you confirm which situation applies?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually Alex has been in hospital for a while now - about 3 months or so. He's been getting treatment there.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to hear that Alex has been in hospital. I hope he's getting better. This does affect the Child Benefit rules, so I need to ask some specific questions.

**Hospital Duration Questions:**

First, let me explain the rule: Child Benefit can continue while a child is in hospital, but there are time limits. The rule looks at **cumulative time** in hospital - meaning if Alex has had multiple hospital stays with gaps of less than 28 days between them, those stays add together.

You mentioned Alex has been in hospital for about 3 months (approximately 12 weeks). 

**Can you tell me:**
1. Has this been one continuous stay of 3 months, or have there been multiple stays?
2. If there have been multiple stays, were the gaps between them less than 28 days?
3. Is the total cumulative time in hospital **more than 12 weeks** or **12 weeks or less**?

This will help me determine how the rules apply to your situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been one continuous stay - he's been there for 12 weeks straight.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Since Alex has been in hospital for exactly 12 weeks, this is right at the limit. There's an important exception to the 12-week rule that could help you:

**Are you regularly spending money on Alex while he is in hospital?**

This could include things like:
- Buying clothes, toys, or personal items for him
- Purchasing treats or entertainment
- Paying for travel to visit him
- Any other regular expenses you incur for his benefit

If you (or your partner, if you're married/in a civil partnership and living together) are regularly spending money on Alex during his hospital stay, you can continue to claim Child Benefit even beyond the 12-week period.

Are you regularly spending money on Alex while he's in hospital?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am. I still buy him things he needs - clothes, books to read, his favorite snacks when I visit. I'm regularly spending on things for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's very good - continuing to spend on Alex regularly means you can continue claiming Child Benefit even though he's been in hospital for 12 weeks.

Now, one final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the local council paying for Alex's care or accommodation costs? If they are, this would generally disqualify you from Child Benefit. If they're not (for example, if NHS hospital care is covering his treatment but the council isn't paying for his accommodation/maintenance), then you would still be eligible.

Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the local council isn't paying anything - it's just NHS hospital care for his treatment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result: ELIGIBLE**

You meet all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for Alex. Even though Alex has been in hospital for 12 weeks (which is at the standard time limit), the fact that you are **regularly spending money on him** during his hospital stay means you can continue to claim Child Benefit beyond this period.

### Summary of Your Eligibility:
✅ Alex is 8 years old (under 16 - qualifying age)  
✅ Alex is not in paid work  
✅ Alex is not receiving benefits in his own right  
✅ Alex is not in an apprenticeship  
✅ You normally live in the UK  
✅ You are responsible for Alex (living with him)  
✅ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex  
✅ You regularly spend money on Alex while he's in hospital  
✅ The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware**: If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge (but you can still claim)
5. **Payments**: Child Benefit is paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### How to Apply:
Visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

I hope Alex recovers well from his treatment. If you have any other questions about your claim, please let me know!

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) Alex is 8 years old (under 16), which is within the qualifying age range; (2) Alex is not working 24+ hours per week; (3) Alex is not receiving benefits in his own right; (4) Alex is not in an apprenticeship; (5) The claimant normally lives in the UK, meeting residency requirements; (6) The claimant lives with Alex and is responsible for him; (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Although Alex has been in hospital for 12 weeks, the claimant is regularly spending money on Alex during his hospital stay, which allows Child Benefit to continue beyond the 12-week limit; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria have been met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for 12 weeks (which is at the limit), the claimant's regular spending on Alex allows them to continue claiming beyond this period. The local council is not contributing to costs, and all other standard eligibility criteria are satisfied.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) Alex is 8 years old (under 16), which is within the qualifying age range; (2) Alex is not working 24+ hours per week; (3) Alex is not receiving benefits in his own right; (4) Alex is not in an apprenticeship; (5) The claimant normally lives in the UK, meeting residency requirements; (6) The claimant lives with Alex and is responsible for him; (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Although Alex has been in hospital for 12 weeks, the claimant is regularly spending money on Alex during his hospital stay, which allows Child Benefit to continue beyond the 12-week limit; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria have been met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for 12 weeks (which is at the limit), the claimant's regular spending on Alex allows them to continue claiming beyond this period. The local council is not contributing to costs, and all other standard eligibility criteria are satisfied.
